title: Normalization vs Standardization
concept: Choose Min-Max scaling for features with strict boundaries (like image pixels) and standardization for models assuming bell curves.

exercises.prompt: Warmup: If a data point is exactly equal to the average (mean) value of a feature, what will its value be after Z-score standardization?
exercises.explain: Exactly! The mean is subtracted from the value, resulting in 0.

cards.title: Suitcase vs. Weather
cards.body: Packing a suitcase requires fitting things within strict physical boundaries (Min-Max). Dressing for the weather is relative—is it warmer or colder than average (Standardization)? They serve different purposes.

cards.title: The Right Scaler
cards.body: Min-Max scaling is best when you need values bounded in a strict range. Standardization is ideal when your algorithm assumes normally distributed data, or when the feature has outliers.

cards.title: Pixels vs. Exams
cards.body: Image models need pixels strictly between 0 and 1, so we normalize. But college admissions compare SAT scores relative to the mean, so they standardize scores.

cards.title: Neural Nets vs. Trees
cards.body: Gradient-based models like linear and logistic regression learn faster and more stably when inputs are standardized (centered at 0). However, tree-based models don't care about scale at all!

cards.title: No universal winner
cards.body: There is no single 'best' scaler. While standardization is often default because it resists outlier distortion, some domains (like computer vision) require normalization.

cards.title: Comparison Summary
cards.body: When to use Normalization vs. Standardization:

left.text:
Strict bounds (e.g. 0 to 1)
Good for bounded inputs
Sensitive to outliers

right.text:
No boundaries, mean = 0
Good for normal curves
Less sensitive to outliers

exercises.prompt: When is Min-Max scaling (normalization) generally preferred over standardization?

options.text: When you need your feature values to lie within a strict, predefined range like 0 to 1
When your dataset contains extreme outliers
When you want the mean of the feature to be exactly 0

exercises.explain: Min-Max scaling guarantees a strict boundary (usually 0 to 1), whereas standardized values can go beyond those bounds.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Standardization is generally less sensitive to extreme outliers than Min-Max scaling.
exercises.explain: True! Min-Max squashes all non-outlier data points close together when a single massive outlier exists, whereas standardization preserves more relative spread.

exercises.sentence: ___ is preferred when training gradient-based models because centering inputs at zero leads to faster, more stable optimization.
exercises.explain: Gradient-based models update weights using gradients; centering inputs at zero with standard deviation of 1 makes optimization more stable.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario is the best fit for Min-Max normalization?

options.text: Preparing pixel brightness values (0 to 255) for an image classification model
Scaling user income in a dataset with multi-millionaire outliers
Formatting house prices for a linear regression model that assumes normally distributed data

exercises.explain: Pixel values have a fixed, strict boundary (0 to 255) and no outliers, making them a perfect candidate for Min-Max normalization.

exercises.prompt: You are training a Support Vector Machine (SVM) model on customer data. The age column has normal distribution, but the income column contains major outliers. If you use Min-Max scaling, the normal incomes will get squished together by the outliers. What should you do?

options.text: Use standardization (Z-score scaling) instead, so the outliers don't compress the normal values into a tiny range.
Keep Min-Max scaling but delete all customer entries with high income.
Do not scale the features at all.
Use target encoding on the income column.

exercises.explain: Correct! Standardization handles outliers much better than Min-Max scaling because it doesn't compress the main cluster of data points. Brilliant decision-making on scaling choices!
